summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that adding a cluster node with an arbitrary URL causes Nginx UI to immediately make HTTP GET requests to that URL. The mock internal service received requests to /api/node and /api/analytic/intro, and the X-Node-Secret header was leaked to the attacker-controlled server.
expected_security_impact: After POST /api/nodes creates a cluster node pointing to http://127.0.0.1:8888, the server should make HTTP requests to the mock service, confirming SSRF. The response body from the internal service is parsed by the server.
actual_observed_result: Running the reproduction built Nginx UI v2.3.3 from the pre-built release binary, installed it, logged in as admin, and POST /api/nodes with url http://127.0.0.1:8888. The mock service received GET /api/node and GET /api/analytic/intro requests with the X-Node-Secret header set to the mock-node-token value.
verification_method: A Python HTTP server on port 8888 logs all incoming requests to /tmp/ssrf_proof.json. The exploit script checks this file for the SSRF_CONFIRMED flag after adding the cluster node. The X-Node-Secret leakage is also confirmed from the proof file.
evidence_produced_by_run: The mock server received GET /api/node HTTP/1.1 with X-Node-Secret: mock-node-token header
The mock server received GET /api/analytic/intro HTTP/1.1 - a second SSRF request triggered by the node analytics subsystem
/tmp/ssrf_proof.json contains SSRF_CONFIRMED: true with full request headers
The server log shows analytic/node_record.go:287 RetrieveNodesStatus start immediately after node creation
GIN logs confirm POST /api/nodes returned 200, triggering the SSRF

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo. The Dockerfile also downloads the pre-built v2.3.3 release binary from GitHub.
